items.prompt_md: Why does binary search work on each key's list of `(timestamp, value)` pairs in this design?
options: Because the dict automatically keeps its keys sorted
Because sets for a given key always arrive in non-decreasing timestamp order, so each key's own list is already sorted
Because the list is re-sorted before every get() call
Because timestamps are always consecutive integers
items.answer: Because sets for a given key always arrive in non-decreasing timestamp order, so each key's own list is already sorted
items.explanation_md: Appending preserves sort order only because the problem guarantees increasing timestamps per key — that guarantee is what makes bisect valid without ever re-sorting.

items.prompt_md: You need to store every value ever set for a key, then later answer "what was this key's value at or before timestamp T?" Which design fits?
options: Overwrite the value on every set, keeping only the latest
A dict mapping key to a plain unsorted list, scanned on every get
A dict mapping key to a list of (timestamp, value) pairs that stays sorted because timestamps only increase, queried with binary search
A single global list of all (key, timestamp, value) triples, scanned on every get
items.answer: A dict mapping key to a list of (timestamp, value) pairs that stays sorted because timestamps only increase, queried with binary search
items.explanation_md: "At or before timestamp T" over a history that only grows forward is the fingerprint for binary search on a per-key sorted list — the dict routes you to the right key, bisect routes you to the right version.

items.prompt_md: For a key with m recorded versions, what is the time complexity of get() in the dict + sorted-list-with-bisect design?
items.explanation_md: bisect on the sorted list of m entries for that key is O(log m); set() is O(1) amortized since it only appends.
